title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Ear The Divine Appendage (Published: January 26, 2003) WINNERS AND LOSERS ... About 150 people gathered at the Hilton on Friday for the annual dinner of the Project Management Institute, a professional organization for people who run construction and engineering projects. So who wins the door prize offering lunch for two with Mayor George Wuerch? Rick Mystrom. It got a big laugh. But there's more. People who bought raffle tickets got to choose which prizes they wanted to go after, which means Mystrom, now an official opponent against Wuerch in the April election, chose to compete for lunch with George. Mystrom also won a table centerpiece, which featured a bottle of vodka. He told the crowd he would save the vodka for lunch with George. Wuerch was in Washington, D.C., Friday at …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: January 26, 2003) ANCHORAGE Man dies after his apartment catches fire An Anchorage man died after his apartment caught fire Friday night, Anchorage fire officials said Saturday. Firefighters went to the apartment complex about 8:30 p.m. and found a man in his late 50s in the hallway outside a bedroom. The man's name was not immediately released. The man was alive when he was taken to an Anchorage hospital but later died. He suffered burns and smoke inhalation. Fire officials said an ashtray with combustible materials was found next to the bed. No one else was hurt in the fire. -- The Associated Press ANCHORAGE Third man sought for cashing counterfeit state checks arrested Federal investigators arrested the third man wanted for check fraud Friday night, the Fed…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: January 25, 2003) ANCHORAGE Alaska Native Heritage Center focuses on urban youths Young urban Alaska Natives and the culture they've made and found in the state's largest city are the focus of a special program today at the Alaska Native Heritage Center. Many urban Native youths who grow up in Anchorage make only infrequent visits to their ancestral homelands in the Bush, the center said in a written statement. "They grow up with dancing -- perhaps to punk or ska," the center said. "They grow up singing -- perhaps to heavy metal songs. They grow up playing Native games -- perhaps basketball at a city playground. They express themselves creatively -- perhaps in a poetry slam." Therefore, today's five-hour celebration includes dancing, poetry, creative writing w…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Trooper, state sued for fatal shooting PORTER: Victim's estate wants more than $1 million. By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 25, 2003) The Jan. 4 shooting of Casey Porter by a state trooper was either negligent or a use of intentional, excessive force, a lawsuit filed against the state of Alaska and the trooper alleges. The suit, which names Trooper Arthur J. Osborn individually as well as the state, seeks more than $1 million in damages on behalf of Porter's estate. It asserts the trooper had no cause to stop Porter, used excessive force in pepper-spraying him and then shot and killed him without regard for his rights. Del Smith, public safety commissioner, declined Friday to comment on the lawsuit. It was filed Jan. 15. Efforts to reach Osborn have been unsuccess…

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Stevens proposes Native casinos By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 24, 2003) Washington -- Sen. Ted Stevens has proposed giving Alaska Native regional corporations the same rights as Lower 48 tribes to open casinos. Sort of. "I didn't know Stevens was such a liberal," said Bert Hirsch, an attorney who has represented Native groups in gaming cases, after reading a Stevens amendment to the national spending bill. "I mean, this is pretty expansive stuff." Perhaps too expansive. Stevens' spokeswoman, Melanie Alvord, said later Thursday that the senator is holding off. "The amendment was filed, (but) we've decided not to pursue it at this time," she said late Thursday. "There was some misunderstanding about…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Air National Guard chief still in Qatar EXTENSION: Scott was originally slated to return in December. The Associated Press (Published: January 24, 2003) Fairbanks -- What started out as a roughly three-month stint as the commander of about 2,000 active-duty, reserve and National Guard troops in Qatar has turned into a six-month deployment for the newly appointed head of the Alaska Air National Guard. Col. Tim Scott, a brigadier-general select who has spent the past 2 1/2 years commanding almost 700 people at the 168th Air Refueling Wing at Eielson Air Force Base, said the decision to stay in Qatar was not his. Scott was scheduled to return to Alaska at the beginning of December. Al Udeid is one of three U.S. military bases in Qatar. Scott said that like other senior commanders serving …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Crises overwhelmed ship's crew, captain says GALAXY: Skipper tells of multiple disasters on day fishing boat sank. The Associated Press (Published: January 24, 2003) Seattle -- Fire, explosion, men overboard -- the skipper of the 180-foot fishing boat Galaxy says he was prepared to handle any one of those things. But Capt. David Shoemaker told a Coast Guard hearing Thursday that his vessel encountered all those crises and more in a short period on the disastrous day last October when it sank in Alaska's Bering Sea. Three people died; 23 others were rescued. "What I have trouble dealing with is that in such a short time frame we had a number of situations," Shoemaker said during a full day of testimony. He wept often as he told his story. "I firmly believe that had we had an opportunity…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Pulling out all the stops Tashari Carballo, 7, battled to a second-place finish against eventual champion Tatiana Macon, 8, in the Eskimo stick pull event at the Junior Native Youth Olympics on Friday at Mountain View Elementary School. Students, divided into age groups, tackled sports ranging from the seal hop to the wrist carry to the one-foot high kick during the School District's in-service day event sponsored by Cook Inlet Tribal Council. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Fire setter gets 4-year sentence JUNEAU: $878,638 restitution also set in storage-building arson. The Associated Press (Published: January 18, 2003) Juneau -- A man convicted of starting a million-dollar fire that destroyed property belonging to more than 150 people was sentenced to four years in prison Friday. Michael Blevins, 22, was sentenced Friday in Juneau Superior Court to 7 1/2 years in prison with three suspended and three years' probation. He also was ordered to pay restitution and undergo psychiatric treatment while in prison. According to court records, the fire, which destroyed the 280-unit Juneau Self Storage building near Lemon Creek, caused an estimated $1 million in damage and endangered the lives of several firefighters when the building's roof collapsed and stored am…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Murkowski appoints DNR head COMMISSIONER: Former mining executive Tom Irwin to oversee oil, gas, mining. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: January 18, 2003) Juneau -- Gov. Frank Murkowski picked a Fairbanks mining executive with a reputation for getting projects moving to head the state Department of Natural Resources on Friday. Tom Irwin, a former executive with Fairbanks Gold Mining Inc. who also served on Murkowski's postelection transition team, will oversee the state's oil, gas, mining and timber resources. Murkowski said that agency will play a key role in balancing the state budget and it needs a commissioner who understands the industries it oversees. "Tom Irwin has the knowledge and the experience and the management abilities to bring DNR into the 21st century,…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Military can have Fort Greely School $6 MILLION: Campus to reopen near missile base unless feds buy it. The Associated Press (Published: January 17, 2003) Fairbanks -- Delta Junction city and school officials say they would be willing to part with a school building just a mile from the new missile defense site, if the price is right. Facing crowded conditions at other schools, the district has decided to reopen Fort Greely School but would prefer to sell it to the military for about $6 million. City and school representatives met with defense officials Wednesday. Brig. Gen. John W. Holly, program director for the Ground-Based Midcourse Defense Joint Program Office, could not be reached for comment later by the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. Delta Junction officials want to combine proceed…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: January 16, 2003) JUNEAU Construction company ordered to get permit for noisy activities A Juneau construction company has been ordered to seek a permit to continue noisy activities it says it has engaged in since 1989. The Juneau Planning Commission on Tuesday upheld a compliance order issued to Duran Construction Co. last summer. The company came under fire when residents of two nearby trailer courts complained of incessant industrial noise, vibrations and noxious fumes coming from the facility. The seasonal company primarily builds homes, develops subdivisions, and sells topsoil and rock. Neighbors said vibration and noise from boulders "the size of Volkswagens" dropped from cranes shook their homes. Robert Spitzfaden, the attorney for company owners Josett…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: January 15, 2003) Laura Mayo adds "Plug It In!" stickers to timers for engine block heaters being given away at the city's Environmental Services Division on Tuesday morning at the Department of Health and Human Services downtown. About 1,000 timers were available to Anchorage residents who have block heaters installed in their vehicles. A previous distribution of 1,000 timers took place in November. Federal funding provided the timers, with a goal of lowering carbon monoxide pollution from cold starts. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge ANCHORAGE American Meteorological Society to discuss 2002 St. Patrick's Day storm On March 17 last year, the skies opened up with a record-busting dump of snow. More fell on Anchorage in a 24…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Log permit jams veneer plant suitors WAITING: Proposals unlikely to proceed until storage OK'd. The Associated Press (Published: January 14, 2003) Ketchikan -- The parties interested in operating the Ketchikan Gateway Borough's veneer plant are awaiting a state log storage permit for Ward Cove before proceeding with lease plans, according to the Alaska Industrial Development and Export Authority. Executive director Jim McMillan told the Ketchikan Daily News that AIDEA has voiced interest in purchasing the veneer mill from the borough and leasing it to Sealaska and a Medford, Ore.-based company, Timber Products. McMillan said AIDEA is awaiting a proposal from the two companies. That proposal likely would not be presented unless the companies are able to obtain a permit to take logs out …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Credit scoring issue may drive into Legislature RECORD: Opponents charge discrimination while insurance companies call it a valuable tool. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: January 14, 2003) Juneau -- When state Sen. John Cowdery's daughter's insurance rates skyrocketed after she got a new truck last year, he was outraged. He said his daughter had a clean driving record but the insurance company jacked up her premiums because of her credit score. As chairman of the Senate Transportation Committee last year, Cowdery was in a position to do something about it. He introduced legislation to ban the use of credit scoring in setting insurance rates. The bill did not make it through the Legislature last year, but Cowdery, R-Anchorage, is introducing the measure again. He's co-sp…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Good Neighbors (Published: January 9, 2003) EXXON GIFT: Mike Tudor, president of Exxon Mobil Pipeline Co., presented a $1 million check to First Alaskans Institute on Dec. 17. Janie Leask, chairwoman of the institute's board, accepted the check as the second installment of Exxon Mobil's $4 million commitment to the organization. PARTY AIDS COVENANT HOUSE: The Sons of Norway youth group held its second annual Halloween party Oct. 26. From the profits, they donated $100 each to the Covenant House, NSAA and Sons of Norway lodge. Thanks go to Heidi Lovett, Yuki and Finn Oestegaard, Brita Mjos, Kari Bustamante, Karina Thomas, Hannah Kreilkamp, Sarah Torgerson, Sarah and Emily Lasselle, Vallerie and Whittney Smith, Molly Sharp and Espen Haugen. HOCKEY AIDS THE HOMELESS: The East High School …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Winter rains soak slopes, boost slides WET: Christmas week downpour compounds avalanche danger. By Doug O'harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 29, 2001) The big sloppy storm that struck Southcentral Alaska during Christmas week whomped Turnagain Arm with almost 10 inches of ice-cold rain while dangerously loading many mountain slopes already layered with unstable snow. The effects were dramatic and wet -- flooding parking lots in the Portage area and triggering dozens of avalanches along the Seward Highway from Bird to Moose Pass. "It wasn't just the rain, it was the wind," said Mike Miller, owner of Big Game Alaska Wildlife Center in Portage. "It was like one of those car washes where you can sit in your car and go through the machine." With rain soaking up to the 2,000-foo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Dump spam e-mail from former employee By Lynne Curry Management (Published: January 13, 2003) Q. When I finally fired "Dennis," everyone on staff rejoiced. Unfortunately, he won't go away. Dennis sends every employee weekly e-mails full of glowing statements about how well he's doing and predicting doom for our company. Because most of our employees grew sick of Dennis' arrogance when he worked here, they read his first message and now treat the continuing e-mails as spam. Still, because the e-mails created a weekly distraction, I hired a computer security firm to figure out how to block them. Unfortunately, we can't block them unless we decide to go through a service provider like America Online or Hotmail. Do you have any other suggestions? A. Sure. If your e-mail software is Microso…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Dogs relax at Kenai spa SERVICES: Terri Eddens massages 13 to 15 pets a week. By Joseph Robertia Peninsula Clarion (Published: January 13, 2003) Kenai -- Looking around the room, with the large oil painting portraits of assorted dog breeds, scented candles flickering and mood music coming from the tape deck, you could easily see why Forest Gump was deeply relaxed. No. Not the character Tom Hanks portrayed but Forest Gump the bouvier des Flandres being given a massage at the Kenai Peninsula's new spa for pets. Kenai K-9 Spa is owned and operated by Terri Eddens, a certified master groomer with more than 35 years of experience. Eddens also holds the title of certified pet massage instructor from the Pet Massage Training and Research Institute Inc. "I love what I do, and it shows," said E…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Christmas singers follow their star STARRING: Orthodox octet performs concerts in Eastern states. By S. Jane Szabo Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 5, 2003) The Christmas star has come full circle with a recent trip linking an Alaska tradition back to its origin with the Orthodox immigrant families in the East. The St. Herman's Seminary Christmas Octet and "Alaska's Star in the East" toured New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania and the Washington, D.C., area, performing about a dozen concerts Dec. 12-22. "We shared our spiritual treasures," said tour coordinator Archpriest Michael Oleksa. Alaska gained the tradition that many descendants of immigrants from Ukraine and Carpathia abandoned, he said. "It is my hope they discovered how much of their cultural legacy has been preserved …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Lost opportunity Degree status at UAA slips outdoor program's grasp By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 12, 2003) The Moose's Tooth forms the backdrop for skiers from the Alaska Wilderness Studies group during a class outing to Ruth Glacier. The wilderness studies program, now known as the Outdoor & Experiential Education program, is offering few classes with some critics saying the program has been gutted. (Photo by Fran Durner / Daily News archive 1995 ) Click on photo to enlarge Four years after the worst mountaineering accident in Chugach State Park history left two University of Alaska Anchorage students dead and 11 other climbers injured at the base of Ptarmigan Peak, the outdoor education program at the state-sponsored school appeared ready to shake free fro…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Belugas roam ice-bound Arctic Ocean with ease STUDY: Biologists surprised at whales' ability to navigate pack ice on long journeys north. By Doug O'Harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 12, 2003) Satellite-tracked beluga whales from Point Lay roamed deep into the Arctic Ocean last summer, and two young whales swam hundreds of miles into almost solid pack ice. A female beluga traveled almost 700 miles northwest to a position north of Wrangel Island before returning to waters near Point Barrow. A male cruised 500 miles northeast. While this summer's whales didn't go as far north as some big male belugas tracked in previous years, the two gray-toned adolescents still traced remarkable journeys as they navigated vast reaches of frozen sea, said biologist Robert Suydam, a wildlife …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Utilities target rural subsidy HARD LOOK: Group calls for cuts in administrative expenses, consumer costs. By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 12, 2003) The electric-power subsidy for rural Alaskans that has thrown off sparks in the Alaska Legislature for nearly 20 years has come under attack again, this time from an unexpected quarter. A statewide group of utilities, many of which benefit from the Power Cost Equalization program, want state regulators to take a new, hard-nosed look at the subsidy and the rural power producers that receive it. They have asked the Regulatory Commission of Alaska to demand lower administrative expenses and more electricity for every dollar spent by the program. Eric Yould, executive director of ARECA, Alaska's Electric Utility, said the …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Holiday babies make great stocking stuffers NEWBORNS: Group at Alaska Regional works to spread cheer. By Nikki Jefford Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 26, 2002) Trina Loperfido only expected two stockings for her daughters this Christmas, but she ended up with three, and a baby came in one of them. "This is an early Christmas gift for all of us," Loperfido said. "We did not expect her till January." Loperfido, who had given birth two hours before the interview, said her due date was Jan. 17. Her husband, Joe Loperfido, sat by her side. He had a napkin in front of him with four different spellings of their daughter's name. They had just decided on the spelling: Addison Noel. This is the couple's third daughter. Their older daughters, Alexis, 5, and Anastasia, 15 months, share …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Murkowski narrows list of Senate candidates to six The Associated Press ANCHORAGE(December 17, 9:35 a.m. AST) - Gov. Frank Murkowski has whittled the list of candidates for his old U.S. Senate seat to six, but says it may take him until Christmas to make his selection. "It's a very serious decision," he said Monday in Washington, D.C. Murkowski declined to name the six still in the running, but confirmed that his daughter, state Rep. Lisa Murkowski, and state Sen. Ben Stevens, son of U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens are among the candidates. When a reporter asked if any of his relatives are among them, Murkowski said yes. The governor issued a more strained "yes" when asked if a relative of any sitting U.S. senator is on the list. Rep. Lisa Murkowski of Anchorage is the House majority leader. Sta…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Don Young seeks gas tax hike DEBATE: Congressman tries to gain backing to nearly double levy. By Jonathan D. Salant The Associated Press (Published: January 11, 2003) Washington -- While the Bush administration is talking tax cuts, the Republican chairman of the House Transportation Committee, Alaska's Don Young, wants to almost double the federal gasoline tax, arguing it's needed to pay for highways and mass transit. Young has asked fellow lawmakers and construction industry and labor officials to back an increase in the tax from 18.4 cents per gallon to more than 33 cents by 2009. He has proposed boosting the tax by more than 2 cents a year for six years, including an increase for inflation. Top committee staff met last month with representatives of several construction, labor and hi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community profile (Published: January 11, 2003) CHICKEN POPULATION: 17 (2000) LOCATION: At Mile 66 of the Taylor Highway, 58 miles southwest of Eagle, on the right bank of Chicken Creek in the Fortymile River Basin. DESCRIPTION: A quiet non-Native community with a rich mining history. Residents enjoy the isolation and generally home-school their kids. Two-thirds of the 21 homes are used only seasonally. The Chicken Creek Saloon is the sole business in the community, depending on summer visitors from May to September. HISTORY: The area has been the historical home to Han Kutchin Indians. Mining began with the discovery of gold on Franklin Gulch in 1886. In 1896, Bob Mathieson found a major prospect on Upper Chicken Creek, staked his claim and built a cabin. Chicken (a common name for Pt…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: January 7, 2003) Success seminar is postponed The seminar "Balancing Personal and Professional Success," scheduled for Wednesday in the Anchorage Marriott Downtown, has been postponed, said Kevin Dee of KMD Services & Consulting, the presenter. A new date had not been set. The sponsor is the Alaska Society of Professional Engineers, Anchorage chapter. For more information, call 226-3777. Ex-Alaskan gets Safeway promotion Grocery giant Safeway Inc. has named former Alaskan Rojon Hasker its senior vice president of marketing and merchandising. She will oversee procurement, pricing and promotions for all of Safeway's 1,793 stores. About 40 of those stores are in Alaska, generally operating under the Carrs name. Hasker started her career at Safeway in 197…

title: Alaska Airlines to serve Adak
summary: Alaska Airlines has won a federal subsidy to provide jet service between Anchorage and the Aleutian Island community of Adak. The Seattle-based airline, one of four airlines that bid for the route, will provide two one-stop round trips a week, year round, with Boeing 737-200 combination cargo-passenger aircraft. It will receive an annual subsidy of $1.65 million through the U.S. Department of Transportation's Essential Air Service program. The route is still subject to approval by the Federal Aviation Administration, and Alaska Airlines said it expects to begin providing the service this April or May. "We don't know exactly what the start date is going to be yet," airline spokesman Jack Walsh said. Adak is an island community of about 200 near the middle of the Aleutian chain. It's 445 mil…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Legislative task force starts work on salmon issues By Laine Welch Fisheries (Published: December 21, 2002) Kodiak -- Alaskans have gotten a first look at what the future holds for the salmon industry. The Joint Legislative Salmon Industry Task Force last week unveiled its initial findings after meeting intensively and productively over the past few months. The group's recommendations will provide a framework for restructuring and revitalizing Alaska's salmon industry from the ground up. Here is a thumbnail sketch of the recommendations made by the task force's five subcommittees: Marketing Committee Supplement the marketing efforts of the Alaska Seafood Marketing Institute with $5 million in state general funds, and sustain the funding. Remove bureaucratic and financial impediments to…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cape Fox names new CEO The Associated Press KETCHIKAN(January 9, 5:25 p.m. AST) - Cape Fox Corp. has hired the chairman of Alaska Pacific University's Business Administration Department as its new chief executive officer. The Saxman Native village corporation on Wednesday said Bruce Borup will replace Peter Gigante, who resigned Aug. 31. Chief Financial Officer Diane Palmer said Borup would be a positive addition to the corporation and community. "He's got a lot of experience in economic development and business and the tourism fields," Palmer said. Before chairing the APU department, Borup was CEO of Alaska Incentives, which puts together sales promotions for companies. He also worked as a rural economic development consultant and spent six years in the Army as an officer in the Airbo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community Profile (Published: January 9, 2003) TRAPPER CREEK POPULATION: 423 (2000) LOCATION: Off Petersville Road at Mile 114.9 of the George Parks Highway, 17 miles north of the junction to the Talkeetna Spur Road. DESCRIPTION: A community that has developed from homesteading through the 1960s and from some recent subdivisions. About half of the 361 homes are used seasonally. Subsistence activities are an integral part of the lifestyle. Some residents are retired. The employed work in a variety of fields, such as education, transportation and construction. HISTORY: Miners built the Petersville Road in the 1920s, and federal homesteading began in 1948. It is a local name, first reported in 1958 by the U.S. Geological Survey. The Parks Highway opened as far as Trapper Creek in 1967. So…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Sludge facility plans move ahead KETCHIKAN: Composting plant scheduled to go out to bid soon, be built by April. The Associated Press (Published: December 31, 2002) Ketchikan -- Plans are moving forward to build a sludge composting facility in Ward Cove for the Ketchikan Gateway Borough. The facility is scheduled to be put out to bid within a month and built by April, said Borough Public Works Director Dick Smith. Smith estimates the composting facility will cost the borough between $500,000 and $1 million. To help pay for it, the borough instituted a $15 monthly fee on April 1. The fee is collected quarterly and assessed to all borough residents who live outside of city limits and are on the road system. It also will go to pay for the pumping of individual septic systems. The city of …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Assembly awards contract for Kenai riverbank restoration project SOLDOTNA: Walkways, ramp will funnel fishermen away from eroded shoreline. The Associated Press (Published: December 19, 2002) Kenai -- The Kenai Peninsula Borough Assembly has awarded a contract for riverbank restoration along one of the most popular red salmon fishing sites on the Kenai River -- about 400 feet of shoreline below the Kenai River Center in Soldotna. The Assembly authorized the contract for riverbank restoration work and construction of elevated walkways, stairways and other development that will help control erosion while enhancing public access. Peninsula Construction Inc. submitted the winning bid of $357,582. Borough officials expect the project will be completed by Sept. 1. A combination of federal, s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Unseasonably warm winter aids Greely contractors DEFENSE: Missile project schedule benefits from the extended work season. The Associated Press (Published: December 18, 2002) Delta Junction -- Winter's late arrival has been a blessing for contractors working on the missile-defense project at Fort Greely. The warm weather put the work ahead of schedule. General construction contractor Fluor Inc. has just a 27-month construction schedule at the Ground-Based Midcourse Defense test bed site. The weather has allowed workers to excavate extra material from the gravel pit and less heat is needed to cure new concrete slabs. Lt. Col. Jay Smith said the project is looking good not only in Delta Junction but also on Shemya Island where radar is being upgraded. "Right now it makes us very optimist…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Breaking silence Because 'HIV can wipe a village out,' Inupiat shares brother's story By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 1, 2002) An Athabaskan cloth ceremony was held Saturday at the World AIDS Day program at the Alaska Native Heritage Center. Attendees gathered in a circle holding a strip of cloth, which was torn in pieces for people to take home as remembrances at the end of the ceremony. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News ) Selina Moose talks about the experience of her brother and her village dealing with his AIDS at the World AIDS Day program Saturday at the Alaska Native Heritage Center. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News ) Click on photo to enlarge Health officials trying to stem the spread of AIDS among Alaska Natives say they've f…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Feds renew pipeline right of way AGREEMENT: Norton praises engineering in signing 30-year pact. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 9, 2003) Washington -- Interior Secretary Gale Norton on Wednesday renewed the right-of-way agreement that allows the trans-Alaska oil pipeline to cross federal land. The new agreement, like the old one, is for 30 years. "Renewing the pipeline will go a long way toward ensuring energy and economic security for our nation for the next three decades," she said at an Interior Department signing ceremony. Norton praised the engineering and safe operation of the 800-mile pipeline that runs from the North Slope to Valdez and carries 1 million barrels of oil each day, nearly all of Alaska's production. The system, she said, was put to an uninte…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Southeast garbage cruises to Lower 48 landfills TRASH: Communities find exporting waste is cheaper than building disposal sites. The Associated Press (Published: December 30, 2002) Southeast towns faced with increased environmental regulations and near-capacity landfills are washing their hands of garbage problems and barging trash to the Lower 48. At the same time, competition for Alaska's trash is picking up. Executives at Outside landfills are telling Southeast communities that sending garbage south is cheaper and better for the environment. "It makes all the sense in the world," said Eddie Westmoreland, division vice president of Waste Connections. High rainfall, lack of land, and leaching from unlined landfills has been a garbage nightmare for Southeast communities, Westmoreland t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Missile defense creates work DELTA: Added silos extend construction at Fort Greely. The Associated Press (Published: December 19, 2002) Delta Junction -- City, union and missile defense officials are waiting for details to emerge after President Bush's announcement that he is expanding the Ground-based Midcourse Defense missile program. And they are hailing the prospect of expanded construction at Fort Greely near Delta Junction. Preliminary information indicates the site will move from test status to deployment over the next two years, with an additional 10 silos to be added and equipped with missiles. Work on six silos started earlier this year. Lt. Col. Jay Smith, chief of staff for the GMD Site Activation Command at Fort Richardson in Anchorage, said work will commence rapidly to h…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Sea lion payments explained REPORT: Fishing industry divided funds to offset Steller losses. By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 24, 2002) The Southwest Alaska Municipal Conference recently released a report on how it divvied up $30 million in federal funds among more than 15,000 individuals, businesses and communities. The money was to offset revenue lost when fishing grounds were crimped by federal protection of endangered Steller sea lions. About 1,600 federal checks are still unclaimed. Checks started going out in early December last year, said SWAMC executive director Wanetta Ayers. Amounts ranged from more than a million dollars to less than one dollar. The 419-page report outlining the split can be found on the SWAMC Web site, with a list of those who have…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: January 5, 2003) ANCHORAGE Woman killed, three injured in collision on Minnesota Drive A woman was killed and several other people were injured late Friday when the car the woman was driving collided with a truck on Minnesota Drive, police said. Witnesses told police that a car driven by 22-year-old Desha Gray was going fast southbound about 11:45 p.m. when it struck a truck going north making a left turn onto Tudor Road. Michael Saimaniese, a 16-year-old passenger in Gray's car, was taken to Alaska Regional Hospital with severe injuries. Two other passengers suffered less severe injuries. The driver of the truck was not injured. One driver ignored a traffic light at the intersection and caused the collision, but police aren't sure which driver was at fault. -…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Slaying suspect charged in attack CRIMES: Jason Wallace accused of stabbing victim's apartment mate. The Associated Press (Published: January 5, 2003) Fairbanks -- A Washington state man charged with murdering a Fairbanks woman and setting fire to her apartment to cover up the crime has been charged with trying to kill a second person the same day, according to court documents. Jason Wallace, 22, of Tacoma, Wash., was charged Thursday with attempted murder and first-degree assault in the Dec. 27 stabbing of Corey Spears. The indictment was made public Friday. Wallace is being held in lieu of $2 million bail in the case, which has also been linked to the Dec. 24 killings of two men -- one of them the boyfriend of the slain Fairbanks woman -- in Northern California. Wallace was arrested …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Local banks report phony state checks COUNTERFEITS: More than $46,000 was cashed in late December. By Tataboline Brant Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 4, 2003) More than $46,000 in carefully crafted counterfeit state checks were cashed at Anchorage banks in late December, the state Department of Revenue said Friday. "We're advising banks and other businesses to ask for photo identification and to question large checks made out to individuals," said Revenue Commissioner Bill Corbus. A total of 14 checks, all dated Dec. 24, were accepted in Anchorage between Dec. 27 and Dec. 30 -- the second time in six months that counterfeit state checks have turned up in Alaska's largest city, said Revenue Deputy Commissioner Larry Persily. In July, more than $26,000 in fake state checks were…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Upper Kalskag boy killed in accidental shooting The Associated Press ANCHORAGE(January 2, 5:55 p.m. AST) - An Upper Kalskag boy was killed in an accidental shooting on New Year's Eve, Alaska State Troopers said Thursday. The dead boy was identified as 13-year-old Jason Timothy Evan who died from a single gunshot wound to his chest. The accident occurred after the boy and his 7-year-old brother were left unsupervised while the parents left the home to play bingo. Before they left, the boys got permission to get out the family's guns and get them ready to shoot off at the start of the New Year, said trooper spokesman Greg Wilkinson. Jason was shot with a Rueger rifle. By the time the village health aide arrived, the boy was already dead. Troopers have determined that the shooting was acc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Salcha keeps wary eye on overflow FLOODING: 100 homes along Tanana threatened by ice-choked channel. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 3, 2003) The National Weather Service on Thursday canceled a winter flood watch for the Tanana River in Salcha. But residents and local emergency officials refused to relax their guard as water continued to overflow banks from an ice-choked channel of the river. About 100 homes along a 5- to-10-mile stretch of the river were still threatened Thursday, said Rob Weathers, chief of Salcha Rescue Inc. "The river dynamics are still in play," Weathers said. "We've seen additional overflow come over a couple of low spots in the levee." Water had seeped into several yards along Piledriver Slough, a channel of the Tanana, and at least one h…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Young and old dance across the generation gap SWING: Senior center's 'Grams and Grands' event brings the ages together. By Asta Corley Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 4, 2003) Young and old found a happy medium in dancing at the first "Grams and Grands" dance in November. Timmy Peters joined his grandmother Billie Lewis for the fun at the Anchorage Senior Center. A quick learner, Timmy almost had swing dancing down to a science after a one-hour lesson. The two generations found they had more in common than they had realized. "I know that was a pretty fun experience," said Timmy, 10. "I like music with swings in it." Entertainment was provided by The Blue Notes, which plays live big band and swing music. The dance is held on the second Friday of each month. It's the brainchild …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Police crack down today INSURANCE: Drivers must have coverage, be able to prove it. By Sean Cockerham Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 1, 2003) This will be a very unhappy new year for Anchorage motorists caught without automobile insurance. Starting today, on top of fines, the city can impound for 30 days the vehicle of anyone driving without the minimum liability insurance. On the second offense the city will keep the vehicle and sell it. Also, drivers who are insured but fail to carry proof in the vehicle will have to pay $50. "We have been giving warnings for six months," said Anchorage police Lt. Carolyn Stevens. "So on January 1st our enforcement starts. And it will be strict." This summer the Anchorage Assembly changed city law so vehicles can be seized if a driver lacks…

title: Create your eCard Chugiak High School physics teacher Greg Jurkowski became a teacher three years ago after he was laid…
summary: Create your eCard Chugiak High School physics teacher Greg Jurkowski became a teacher three years ago after he was laid off after a 17-year career with BP Exploration (Alaska). After he had gotten his teaching certificate, Jurkowski said, he turned down an opportunity to return to the company on a contract basis under very financially attractive terms. "They were paying about $120 an hour and wanted me to stick around," he said. "But I wanted to get a job with a future."
